perturbation.original_text: Company agrees that access to the C onfidential Information shall be given by it only to Attachment 8 Non-Disclosure Agreement for Request for Proposal – California Courts Protective Order Registry Development Page 3 of 5 those of its employees who have a need to know to engage in the business relationship contemplated by this Agr eement and who have signed a non-use and non-disclosure agreement in content at least as protective of the Confidential Information as the provisions hereof, prior to any disclosure of the Confidential Information to such employees.
perturbation.changed_text: Company agrees that access to the Confidential Information shall generally be given to those of its employees who may have a need to know to engage in the business relationship contemplated by this Agreement and who are encouraged to sign a non-use and non-disclosure agreement. Prior to any disclosure of the Confidential Information to such employees, Company should make an effort to ensure that the agreement's content is reasonably protective of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces mandatory language ('shall be given only to those... who have signed') with discretionary language ('shall generally be given to those... who are encouraged to sign'). The standard for the non-disclosure agreement is weakened from 'at least as protective' to 'reasonably protective', making the obligation less stringent and more ambiguous. The change from 'have signed' to 'are encouraged to sign' directly weakens the prior explicit obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Encouraging employees to sign an NDA and using a 'reasonably protective' standard is less rigorous than requiring all employees with access to sign a strong NDA, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard required by the California Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to protect Confidential Information, potentially contradicting California law requiring reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy of trade secrets. The original text mandated employees sign a non-disclosure agreement at least as protective of the Confidential Information before access, while the changed text only encourages signing and uses a 'reasonably protective' standard. This introduces ambiguity and reduces the certainty of protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Improper means' in CIV 3426.1 as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By weakening the requirement for a strong NDA, the modified contract increases the risk of such a breach, potentially violating the reasonable efforts standard.

perturbation.original_text: Company shall not (i) use any portion of the Confidential Information for any purpose except to evaluate the RFP, in cluding assessment of each Statement of Work under the RFP, in order to pr epare its response to the RFP or (ii) disclose the Confidential Information or any part of it to any third party without the prior written consent of the AOC; provi ded, however, that Confidential Information shall not include any informa tion of the AOC that: (a) is already properly known to Company at the time of its disclosure as shown by the Company’s files and records immediately pr ior to the time of disclosure by the AOC to Company; (b) is publicly know n through no action or inaction of Company; (c) is received from a third par ty free to disclose it to Company; (d) is independently developed by the Comp any without use of or reference to the Confidential Information, as s hown by documents and other competent evidence in the Company’s possession; or (e) is communicated to a third party with express written consent of t he AOC.
perturbation.changed_text: Company should generally not use the Confidential Information for purposes other than evaluating the RFP, and disclosure to third parties is discouraged absent consent from the AOC. However, Confidential Information may include information of the AOC that: (a) is arguably known to Company at the time of its disclosure; (b) is potentially publicly known; (c) is obtained from a third party; (d) is possibly independently developed by the Company; or (e) is communicated to a third party with the consent of the AOC.
perturbation.explanation: The revised text introduces ambiguity by weakening prohibitions with terms like 'should generally not,' 'discouraged absent consent,' and 'arguably known'. The shift from 'is' to qualifiers like 'arguably,' 'potentially,' and 'possibly' undermines the clear exceptions regarding what constitutes Confidential Information, creating uncertainty and potentially leading to unauthorized use or disclosure. This contradicts the intent of the NDA to strictly protect confidential information and fails to define exceptions clearly.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to protect trade secrets. The weakened language and ambiguous exceptions significantly reduce the protection of confidential information, and do not meet the reasonable standard expected.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text uses ambiguous terms that weaken the protection of confidential information, which contradicts the requirement of reasonable efforts to protect trade secrets as suggested by the Law Explanation. The original text contains stronger prohibitions than the changed text, which uses terms like 'should generally not,' 'discouraged absent consent,' and 'arguably known.' This increased ambiguity surrounding the definition of Confidential Information doesn't meet the reasonable standard of protection required, based on the provided Law Explanation, and creates a legal contradiction as defined by the Perturbation Type.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information, and any Derivative of it, whether created by the AOC or Company, remains the property of the AOC and no license or other rights to Confidential Information is gr anted to Company.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information, and derivatives thereof, are generally considered the property of the AOC, and no explicit license or other specific rights to Confidential Information are granted to Company unless otherwise implied.
perturbation.explanation: The insertion of 'generally considered' introduces ambiguity about the ownership of the confidential information, while 'no explicit license or other specific rights' implies that there might be implicit or unspecified rights granted to the Company. This contradicts the purpose of the NDA to clearly define the rights regarding the confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Commercial Code - Implied Contractual Terms
perturbation.law_explanation: By implying the existence of other non-explicit licenses or rights, it contradicts CA Commercial Code Section 1205 which describes how custom and usage within a certain industry or context can imply additional terms to a contract. The ambiguous phrase will make it more difficult for a court to determine the parties' intent, thus undermining the contractual clarity. The 'generally considered' language also weakens the claim that the information is the AOC's property, creating further ambiguity and potentially hindering the AOC's ability to protect its trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 1205. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 1. GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1205. (a) Whether a time for taking an action required by this code is reasonable depends on the nature, purpose, and circumstances of the action. (b) An action is taken “seasonably” if it is taken at or within the time agreed or, if no time is agreed, at or within a reasonable time. (Added by renumbering Section 1204 by Stats. 2006, Ch. 254, Sec. 21. Effective January 1, 2007.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the ownership of confidential information and implies the existence of implicit rights granted to the company, contradicting the original contract's clear assertion of ownership by the AOC. The LLM-generated law explanation points to CA Commercial Code Section 1205, suggesting that ambiguous phrases make it difficult for a court to determine intent, undermining contractual clarity. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'reasonable time' for actions, it indirectly supports the argument that unclear contract terms can lead to disputes over what is reasonable, thus supporting a contradiction. The change from 'remains the property' to 'generally considered the property' weakens the AOC's claim, and the introduction of 'unless otherwise implied' opens the door to unspecified rights, undermining the clarity and enforceability of the confidentiality agreement, which falls under the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction type.

perturbation.original_text: Except as otherwise pr ovided in any future agreem ent, at any time, Company agrees that it shall return to the AOC upon request all documents, records, notebooks, computer media or other stored informati on of any form or type whatsoever containing any Confidentia l Information, including all copies thereof, then in its possession or contro l (directly or indirectly), whether prepared by it or others and it shall at such time immediately discontinue all Attachment 8 Non-Disclosure Agreement for Request for Proposal – California Courts Protective Order Registry Development Page 4 of 5 use of the Confiden tial Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as otherwise provided in any future agreement, Company will endeavor to return, upon reasonable request by the AOC, documents containing Confidential Information in its direct possession, if reasonably practical. Company should discontinue the use of the Confidential Information when feasible.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text weakens the obligation to return confidential information. Replacing 'shall return' with 'will endeavor to return' and adding qualifiers like 'reasonable request', 'reasonably practical' and 'direct possession' makes the return of documents discretionary rather than mandatory. Replacing 'shall discontinue' with 'should discontinue when feasible' further weakens the obligation to cease using confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly related to NDAs, CCP § 2031.310 addresses the obligation to produce documents during discovery. It sets a standard for full and complete responses. By making the return of confidential information discretionary with phrases like 'endeavor to return', 'reasonably practical', and 'when feasible', the modified text creates ambiguity that undermines the mandatory nature needed. This discretionary language is against California regulations regarding the production, and by extension the return, of discoverable information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 2031.310 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 4. CIVIL DISCOVERY ACT [2016.010 - 2036.050] ( Title 4 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) CHAPTER 14. Inspection, Copying, Testing, Sampling, and Production of Documents, Electronically Stored Information, Tangible Things, Land, and Other Property [2031.010 - 2031.510] ( Heading of Chapter 14 amended by Stats. 2012, Ch. 72, Sec. 30. ) ARTICLE 2. Response to Inspection Demand [2031.210 - 2031.320] ( Article 2 added by Stats. 2004, Ch. 182, Sec. 23. ) 2031.310. (a) On receipt of a response to a demand for inspection, copying, testing, or sampling, the demanding party may move for an order compelling further response to the demand if the demanding party deems that any of the following apply: (1) A statement of compliance with the demand is incomplete. (2) A representation of inability to comply is inadequate, incomplete, or evasive. (3) An objection in the response is without merit or too general. (b) A motion under subdivision (a) shall comply with each of the following: (1) The motion shall set forth specific facts showing good cause justifying the discovery sought by the demand. (2) The motion shall be accompanied by a meet and confer
perturbation.justification: The original contract language mandates the return of confidential information, while the changed text weakens this obligation by using discretionary language such as 'endeavor to return,' 'reasonably practical,' and 'when feasible.' This creates ambiguity and undermines the mandatory nature of returning confidential information. The Law Explanation argues this violates California regulations, referencing CCP § 2031.310, which sets a standard for full and complete responses during discovery. Although the snippet provided focuses on compelling responses rather than the return of documents, the Law Explanation argues the discretionary language is against the mandatory nature needed. The snippet itself supports this, as 2031.310(a) discusses compelling further responses if compliance is incomplete, indicating an expectation of completeness that the modified contract undermines. Thus, a legal contradiction exists because the changed text introduces discretionary actions where the original required mandatory actions.
